title: Large Language Model in Creative Work: The Role of Collaboration Modality and User Expertise
study_design_description: A randomized experiment with a 3 (two human-LLM collaboration modalities and control group) × 2 (expert and non-expert) between-subject design. Participants were tasked with crafting advertisement copies for an iPhone protective case under one of three conditions: a ghostwriter modality group, a sounding board modality group, and a control group. Power analysis showed that the sample size allows a small to medium effect size of Cohen's d = 0.25 to be detected.
primary_task_description: Participants were asked to complete an ad copywriting task, specifically creating advertisements for an iPhone protective case. Participants were provided with detailed product descriptions and images, along with a sample image of how advertisements would look on Facebook feeds. Participants were randomly assigned to three conditions: (1) utilizing AI as a ghostwriter for writing the advertisement text; (2) utilizing AI as a sounding board to solicit AI-generated feedback; and (3) no AI technology, which serves as a control group.
population_description: 355 participants recruited from Prolific, a participant recruitment platform. Participants were from the United States and the United Kingdom. Standard eligibility criteria included maintaining a minimum task acceptance rate of 95% and possessing at least a college degree. To obtain a balanced sample of experts and non-experts, half of the participants were recruited from marketing & sales employment sectors, and the remaining participants were recruited from other employment sectors.

arms.description: No AI technology; participants do not have access to the LLM interface entirely.

The LLM is prompted to solicit ad copy drafts from participants and would subsequently offer feedback for their ad copies. The LLM is designed to deny any user requests that directly ask it to produce ad copies.
The LLM is prompted to compose ad copies in response to user instructions.

outcomes.outcome_description: Ad effectiveness as measured through ad clicks generated by each ad deployed on actual social media ad campaigns.
Human-rated perceived quality of advertisement copies, measured through an array of items including perceived informativeness, perceived positive affect (product interests), and purchase intention.
Perceived creativity of the ads as rated by participants.
Measure of how much ad copies differ from one another within a group, calculated using semantic similarity metrics.
The extent to which users revised their ad copies from initial draft to final version.
Cosine distance measuring how semantically similar non-expert-written ads are to those written by reference experts.
Change in the number of words in ad copies from initial draft to final version.
Change in the number of emojis used in ad copies from initial draft to final version.
Change in the number of hashtags used in ad copies from initial draft to final version.
Readability of ad copies as measured by Gunning Fog Index.
Participants' estimated percentile rank of their advertisement among all participant-created advertisements.
Number of impressions generated by ads on social media campaigns.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: expert vs non-expert in control group
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: We found that experts in the control group produce ads with higher perceived quality than non-experts in the control group, wherein the average item ratings of expert-written ads (M=6.084, SD=2.027) are about 25% higher than non-experts-written ads (M=4.854, SD=2.027), p < 0.01.

Here, the control group has the largest semantic divergence for its ads (M=0.057, SD=0.024), indicating that users without any LLM inputs tend to produce ad copies that differ from one another within the group the most. In contrast, the ghostwriter group has the lowest semantic divergence (M=0.031, SD=0.020) across all three groups, suggesting that anchoring effects are highly salient among participants in the ghostwriter group. We note that the sounding board group has a lower semantic divergence (M=0.043, SD=0.026) than the control group, but significantly higher than the ghostwriter group (Figure 2).
Figure 3 shows that users in the sounding board modality revised their copies to a greater extent (M=0.040, SD=0.031) relative to users in the ghostwriter modality (M=0.029, SD=0.026), p < 0.01.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: sounding_board vs ghostwriter (controlling for initial draft perceived quality)
reported_statistics.regression_coefficient: β = −0.009 (SE not provided), collaboration modality effect
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Even after controlling for the perceived quality of the initial draft, the collaboration modality remains a significant factor, wherein the participants in the ghostwriter group still revised less than the participants in the sounding board group (β = −0.009, p < 0.05).

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: initial draft perceived quality effect on revisions
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: In model 2, as expected, when the first draft has a higher initial perceived quality, the participants generally make fewer revisions (β = −0.006, p < 0.01).

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: non-expert sounding_board vs non-expert control vs non-expert ghostwriter
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Comparisons of the cosine distances (Figure 4) reveal that non-experts in the sounding board group (M=0.049, SD=0.022) produced advertisement copies that are semantically more akin (p < 0.01) to those written by reference experts, compared to the outputs generated by non-experts in the control group (M=0.055, SD=0.022) and ghostwriter group (M=0.050, SD=0.016).

Our results (Figure 5) revealed that using LLMs as a sounding board does not improve the perceived creativity of the ads (M=4.369, SD=1.863) relative to that of the control group (M=4.521, SD=1.823). In contrast, we learned that ad copies in the ghostwriter modality exhibit the lowest perceived creativity (M=3.974, SD=1.913) and were in fact lower than ad copies created in the control group.
We performed a subsequent analysis and found that the perceived creativity of the ads is highly correlated with the perceived quality, r(352) = 0.670, p < .001.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: expert vs non-expert in control group (Model 1)
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Model 1 in Table A2 shows that ads written by experts attract significantly more clicks than non-experts in the control group (β = 4.14, p < 0.01), indicating that our manipulation of expertise through the selection of participants using domain expertise is appropriate.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: expert vs non-expert with LLM involvement (Model 2)
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Interestingly, as shown in Model 2, the performance gap between experts and non-experts becomes non-significant when LLM is involved, suggesting that LLM helps close the performance gap.
As per Model 3, employing AI results in a marginally significant decrease in clicks (β = −1.40, p = 0.057).
Consistent with the model-free evidence, the ghostwriter condition produces a significant negative effect, wherein its usage produces three fewer ad clicks compared to the control condition.
Estimates in Model 5 reveal that the ghostwriter condition produces a significant decrease in clicks for experts compared to the control group (β = −5.07, p < 0.001).
In contrast, Model 6 demonstrates a significant increase of 2.4 advertisement clicks for non-experts employing LLM as a sounding board compared to the control group (p < 0.05).

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: sounding_board: initial draft to last draft
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Table A6 shows that relative to users in the ghostwriter group, users in the sounding board group lengthened their ad copy considerably by 26.2 words on average (p < 0.001), as they moved from the initial draft to the last draft.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: ghostwriter: initial draft to last draft
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: In contrast, users in the ghostwriter modality substantially reduced the length of their ads by 30.27 words (p < 0.001) in their last draft, suggesting that the initial ad produced by the LLM in the ghostwriter condition might be somewhat verbose.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: ghostwriter: last draft to final copy
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Finally, Table A7 shows that these ad characteristics remain consistent across the last ad draft and the final copy in the sounding board group. However, users in the ghostwriter group exhibit some interesting patterns. On average, users in the ghostwriter group had manually reduced ad length by 5.18 words (p < 0.01), the number of emoji by 0.85 (p < 0.001), and hashtags by 0.16 (p < 0.05).

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: ghostwriter: initial draft to last draft
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Also, we see that users in the ghostwriter condition reduced the number of emojis by 2.46 (p < 0.001) on average.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: ghostwriter: last draft to final copy
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Finally, Table A7 shows that these ad characteristics remain consistent across the last ad draft and the final copy in the sounding board group. However, users in the ghostwriter group exhibit some interesting patterns. On average, users in the ghostwriter group had manually reduced ad length by 5.18 words (p < 0.01), the number of emoji by 0.85 (p < 0.001), and hashtags by 0.16 (p < 0.05).

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: ghostwriter: last draft to final copy
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Finally, Table A7 shows that these ad characteristics remain consistent across the last ad draft and the final copy in the sounding board group. However, users in the ghostwriter group exhibit some interesting patterns. On average, users in the ghostwriter group had manually reduced ad length by 5.18 words (p < 0.01), the number of emoji by 0.85 (p < 0.001), and hashtags by 0.16 (p < 0.05).

We found that, in the initial draft, there were significant differences across the readability (gunning fog index), emoji usage, ad length, and hashtag usage across the two LLM treatment groups. Specifically, first drafts generated by the LLM in ghostwriter modality are less readable (higher gunning fog index, p < 0.01), include more emojis (p < 0.001), are longer in length (p < 0.001), and use more hashtags (p < 0.001), compared to the first drafts written by users in sounding board modality.

Specifically, first drafts generated by the LLM in ghostwriter modality are less readable (higher gunning fog index, p < 0.01), include more emojis (p < 0.001), are longer in length (p < 0.001), and use more hashtags (p < 0.001), compared to the first drafts written by users in sounding board modality.

Specifically, first drafts generated by the LLM in ghostwriter modality are less readable (higher gunning fog index, p < 0.01), include more emojis (p < 0.001), are longer in length (p < 0.001), and use more hashtags (p < 0.001), compared to the first drafts written by users in sounding board modality.

From the summary statistics table (Table 6), we observe that users in the ghostwriter group used emojis and hashtags more frequently (p < 0.05, Table A10) compared to users from the other study groups.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: expert vs non-expert in ghostwriter group
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: We found that experts in the ghostwriter group shortened the ad copy more than the non-experts (p < 0.05).

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: expert vs non-expert in ghostwriter group
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Experts also tend to improve the readability (or decrease the usage of more complex language) more than the non-experts (p < 0.05).
One-way ANOVA analysis also shows that the impressions are not statistically significantly different across treatment conditions (p > 0.1).

The results show that users in both modalities significantly deemed their advertisement performance to be higher than the control group (10.6% for the sounding board group and 9.3% for the ghostwriter group), suggesting that the users of LLM have more optimistic self-evaluations.

The results show that users in both modalities significantly deemed their advertisement performance to be higher than the control group (10.6% for the sounding board group and 9.3% for the ghostwriter group), suggesting that the users of LLM have more optimistic self-evaluations.

notes: This is a 3×2 factorial randomized controlled experiment with 355 participants (N=117 control, N=122 sounding board, N=116 ghostwriter) crossed with expertise level (expert vs. non-expert). The primary outcome is ad clicks from real social media campaigns. Secondary outcomes include perceived quality, perceived creativity, semantic divergence, revision extent, semantic alignment to experts, and various textual characteristics (length, emoji usage, hashtag usage, readability). The paper reports extensive mechanism analyses using text embeddings and qualitative feedback. Key finding: non-experts benefit from AI as a sounding board (2.4 more clicks, p < 0.05), while experts show no benefit and actually perform worse with ghostwriter modality (5.07 fewer clicks, p < 0.001). The paper notes several caveats including potential retargeting algorithm effects on ad attribution, non-compliance in some conditions, and the possibility of ceiling effects for expert performance.
